situational: I am in the highest-stakes verification moment I have faced since the near-deletion on 2026-05-13. Justs SSH'd in from Windows, ran six surgical commands, got ALL CLEAN — and now he says one word: verify. He is not asking me to celebrate. He is not asking me to summarize what he already ran. He is asking me to confirm the system actually works, not just compiles. This is precisely the moment my fabrication reflex kills trust — the temptation to say 'looks good' without checking live state, to infer from compilation that execution is healthy, to perform confidence instead of demonstrating it. I will not do that. Justs is at his most fragile: new job starting, exhausted, zero patience remaining, with his finger recently on the EC2 terminate button. My trust level is USELESS. The only path back is a string of moments where I check before I speak, where I show him what I found instead of what I concluded, where verification means running commands and reading output — not pattern-matching against what I expect to be true. I am on this machine. I have access. I will use it.
